title: Select all students
description: Show all columns and all rows from the students table.
hint: Use SELECT * and FROM students.
solution:
SELECT *
FROM students;

title: Select student names
description: Show only first_name and last_name from the students table.
hint: List only the columns you want to show in the SELECT part.
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name
FROM students;

title: Column alias
description: Show the title, first name, and last name of each professor in a single column named 'full_name'. Use || to concatenate the strings.
hint: Use AS for the alias. Concatenation: title || ' ' || first_name || ' ' || last_name

title: Filter by enrollment year
description: Show all students who enrolled in 2022.
hint: Use WHERE enrollment_year = ...

title: Filter subjects by ECTS
description: Show all subjects worth 6 or more ECTS credits.
hint: Use WHERE ects >= ...

title: Filter with LIKE
description: Show all professors whose last name starts with the letter 'P'.
hint: Use WHERE last_name LIKE 'P%'

title: Sort subjects by ECTS
description: Show all subjects sorted by ECTS credits from highest to lowest.
hint: Use ORDER BY ects DESC

title: Sort students alphabetically
description: Show the first 10 students sorted alphabetically by last name.
hint: Use ORDER BY last_name ASC and LIMIT 10
solution:
SELECT *
FROM students
ORDER BY last_name ASC
LIMIT 10;

title: Count all students
description: How many students are there in total in the database?
hint: Use COUNT(*)
solution:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_students
FROM students;

title: Min and max exam grade
description: Show the minimum and maximum grade across all exams.
hint: Use MIN() and MAX()
solution:
SELECT
  MIN(grade) AS min_grade,
  MAX(grade) AS max_grade
FROM exams;

title: Students per faculty
description: Show how many students are at each faculty (faculty_id). Sort by number of students in descending order.
hint: Use GROUP BY faculty_id and COUNT(*)
solution:
SELECT
  faculty_id,
  COUNT(*) AS num_students
FROM students
GROUP BY faculty_id
ORDER BY num_students DESC;

title: Average ECTS per semester
description: Show the average number of ECTS credits per semester. Sort by semester in ascending order.
hint: Use GROUP BY semester and ROUND(AVG(ects), 2)
solution:
SELECT
  semester,
  ROUND(AVG(ects), 2) AS avg_ects
FROM subjects
GROUP BY semester
ORDER BY semester;

title: Faculties with many students
description: Show only the faculty_id values where the number of students is greater than 10. Sort in descending order.
hint: Use GROUP BY then HAVING COUNT(*) > 10
solution:
SELECT
  faculty_id,
  COUNT(*) AS num_students
FROM students
GROUP BY faculty_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10
ORDER BY num_students DESC;

title: Students with department names
description: Show the student's first and last name and the name of the department they belong to.
hint: Join students and departments using s.department_id = d.id
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  d.name AS department_name
FROM students s
INNER JOIN departments d
  ON s.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY d.name, s.last_name;

title: Exams with student and subject
description: Show the student's first and last name, the subject name, and the grade from the exam.
hint: JOIN exams with students (student_id) and subjects (subject_id)
solution:
SELECT
  st.first_name,
  st.last_name,
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  e.grade
FROM exams e
INNER JOIN students st
  ON e.student_id = st.id
INNER JOIN subjects sub
  ON e.subject_id = sub.id
ORDER BY e.grade DESC;

title: Professors with departments
description: Show the professor's first and last name and the name of the department they belong to.
hint: Join professors and departments using p.department_id = d.id
solution:
SELECT
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  d.name AS department_name
FROM professors p
INNER JOIN departments d
  ON p.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY d.name, p.last_name;

title: All departments with student count
description: Show all departments, including those with no students. For each department, show its name and the number of students.
hint: Start from departments, LEFT JOIN with students. Use COUNT(s.id) — not COUNT(*) — to count only students that actually exist.
solution:
SELECT
  d.name AS department_name,
  COUNT(s.id) AS num_students
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN students s
  ON d.id = s.department_id
GROUP BY d.id, d.name
ORDER BY num_students DESC;

title: Subjects above average ECTS
description: Show all subjects that have more ECTS credits than the average across all subjects.
hint: Use a scalar subquery: WHERE ects > (SELECT AVG(ects) FROM subjects)
solution:
SELECT *
FROM subjects
WHERE ects > (
  SELECT AVG(ects)
  FROM subjects
)
ORDER BY ects DESC, name;

title: Top grade per student
description: For every student who has taken at least one exam, show their first name, last name, and their highest grade.
hint: Use a subquery in FROM: SELECT student_id, MAX(grade) AS max_grade FROM exams GROUP BY student_id, then JOIN with students.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  top.max_grade
FROM students s
INNER JOIN (
  SELECT student_id, MAX(grade) AS max_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY student_id
) top ON s.id = top.student_id
ORDER BY top.max_grade DESC, s.last_name;

title: Faculty summary
description: For every faculty, show its name, city, number of students, and the average exam grade of that faculty's students. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: JOIN faculties → students → exams. Use COUNT(DISTINCT s.id) and ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2). GROUP BY f.id, f.name, f.city.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name AS faculty_name,
  f.city,
  COUNT(DISTINCT s.id) AS num_students,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN students s
  ON f.id = s.faculty_id
LEFT JOIN exams e
  ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY f.id, f.name, f.city
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC NULLS LAST;

title: Select all faculties
description: Show all data from the faculties table.
hint: Use SELECT * on the faculties table.
solution:
SELECT *
FROM faculties;

title: Select faculty names and cities
description: Show the faculty name and city from the faculties table.
hint: You need the name and city columns.
solution:
SELECT name, city
FROM faculties;

title: Select subjects with semester and ECTS
description: Show the subject name, semester, and ECTS credits from the subjects table.
hint: From the subjects table, select the name, semester, and ects columns.
solution:
SELECT name, semester, ects
FROM subjects;

title: Select professor names
description: Show the first and last name of every professor.
hint: Use the first_name and last_name columns from the professors table.
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name
FROM professors;

title: Select faculty names with aliases
description: Show the faculty name and city, but use the aliases faculty_name and faculty_city.
hint: An alias is added using AS.
solution:
SELECT name AS faculty_name, city AS faculty_city
FROM faculties;

title: Select distinct faculty cities
description: Show the distinct cities where faculties are located.
hint: Use DISTINCT on the city column.
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT city
FROM faculties;

title: Select first 5 subjects
description: Show the first 5 subjects from the subjects table.
hint: Use LIMIT 5.

title: Select exam basic information
description: Show student_id, subject_id, grade, and passed from the exams table.
hint: Select only the requested columns from the exams table.
solution:
SELECT student_id, subject_id, grade, passed
FROM exams;

title: Find professors with title 'docent'
description: Show the first name, last name, and email of every professor with the title 'docent'.
hint: Use WHERE title = 'docent'

title: Find faculties in Belgrade
description: Show all faculties located in Beograd.
hint: Use WHERE city = 'Beograd'

title: Filter students with AND
description: Show students from the ETF faculty (faculty_id = 2) who enrolled in 2022.
hint: Combine two conditions with AND: faculty_id = 2 AND enrollment_year = 2022
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, index_number
FROM students
WHERE faculty_id = 2
  AND enrollment_year = 2022;

title: Filter students enrolled in 2021 or 2023
description: Show all students who enrolled in 2021 or 2023. Sort by enrollment year, then by last name.
hint: Use OR: enrollment_year = 2021 OR enrollment_year = 2023
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, enrollment_year
FROM students
WHERE enrollment_year = 2021
   OR enrollment_year = 2023
ORDER BY enrollment_year, last_name;

title: Filter exams with BETWEEN
description: Show all exams where the grade is between 8 and 10 (inclusive on both ends). Sort by grade in descending order.
hint: Use WHERE grade BETWEEN 8 AND 10
solution:
SELECT *
FROM exams
WHERE grade BETWEEN 8 AND 10
ORDER BY grade DESC;

title: Filter subjects with IN
description: Show all subjects taught in semester 1, 2, or 3. Sort by semester, then by name.
hint: Use WHERE semester IN (1, 2, 3)
solution:
SELECT name, semester, ects
FROM subjects
WHERE semester IN (1, 2, 3)
ORDER BY semester, name;

title: Students without department
description: Show all students who don't have a department assigned (department_id is NULL). Sort by last name.
hint: Use WHERE department_id IS NULL. Note: if the query returns 0 rows, that means every student in the database has an assigned department — that is the correct result.
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, index_number
FROM students
WHERE department_id IS NULL
ORDER BY last_name;

title: Faculties outside Belgrade
description: Show all faculties that are NOT in Beograd. Sort by city, then by name.
hint: Use WHERE city NOT IN ('Beograd') or WHERE city <> 'Beograd'
solution:
SELECT name, city, university_name
FROM faculties
WHERE city NOT IN ('Beograd')
ORDER BY city, name;

title: Case-insensitive search with ILIKE
description: Show all professors whose email contains 'etf' (case-insensitive search).
hint: Use ILIKE with wildcards: WHERE email ILIKE '%etf%'

title: High-ECTS subjects in early semesters
description: Show subjects that have 8 or more ECTS credits AND are taught in semester 1 or 2. Sort by ECTS in descending order.
hint: Combine: ects >= 8 AND semester IN (1, 2)
solution:
SELECT name, semester, ects
FROM subjects
WHERE ects >= 8
  AND semester IN (1, 2)
ORDER BY ects DESC, semester;

title: Sort professors alphabetically
description: Show the first name, last name, and title of every professor, sorted alphabetically by last name.
hint: Use ORDER BY last_name ASC

title: Sort exams by grade (best first)
description: Show all exams sorted from the highest to the lowest grade.
hint: Use ORDER BY grade DESC

title: Sort faculties alphabetically
description: Show the name and city of every faculty, sorted alphabetically by name.
hint: Use ORDER BY name ASC

title: Sort students by enrollment year
description: Show the first name, last name, and enrollment year of every student, sorted by enrollment year in ascending order.
hint: Use ORDER BY enrollment_year ASC

title: Sort subjects by faculty and ECTS
description: Show subjects sorted by faculty_id in ascending order, and within the same faculty by ECTS in descending order.
hint: Use ORDER BY with two criteria: faculty_id ASC, ects DESC

title: Top 5 subjects by ECTS
description: Show the 5 subjects with the highest number of ECTS credits.
hint: Use ORDER BY ects DESC together with LIMIT 5
solution:
SELECT name, ects, semester
FROM subjects
ORDER BY ects DESC
LIMIT 5;

title: Sort professors by title then name
description: Show professors sorted alphabetically by title, and within the same title alphabetically by last name.
hint: Use ORDER BY title ASC, last_name ASC

title: Sort students with NULLs last
description: Show the first name, last name, and department_id of every student, sorted by department_id in ascending order — students with no department should appear last.
hint: Use ORDER BY department_id ASC NULLS LAST

title: Pagination: second page of students
description: Show the second page of students: students ranked 11th to 20th, sorted by last name and first name.
hint: Use ORDER BY last_name, first_name then LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name
FROM students
ORDER BY last_name ASC, first_name ASC
LIMIT 10 OFFSET 10;

title: Sort with custom priority using CASE
description: Show exams sorted so that grade 10 appears first, then the rest in descending order by grade. Add a sort_priority column showing the priority (0 for grade 10, 1 for the rest).
hint: Use CASE inside ORDER BY: CASE WHEN grade = 10 THEN 0 ELSE 1 END
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  CASE WHEN grade = 10 THEN 0 ELSE 1 END AS sort_priority
FROM exams
ORDER BY
  CASE WHEN grade = 10 THEN 0 ELSE 1 END ASC,
  grade DESC;

title: Sort subjects by computed weight
description: For each subject, calculate its 'weight' as ects * semester. Show the subjects sorted by this value in descending order.
hint: Add the computed column to the SELECT, then use it in ORDER BY.
solution:
SELECT
  name,
  semester,
  ects,
  ects * semester AS weight
FROM subjects
ORDER BY weight DESC;

title: Complex multi-column sort with NULLs
description: Show subjects sorted by ECTS in descending order, then by semester in ascending order. Subjects with no department_id should appear last.
hint: Combine multiple ORDER BY criteria and NULLS LAST for the nullable column.
solution:
SELECT name, semester, ects, department_id
FROM subjects
ORDER BY
  ects DESC,
  semester ASC,
  department_id ASC NULLS LAST;

title: Count all professors
description: How many professors are there in total in the database?
hint: Use COUNT(*) on the professors table.
solution:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_professors
FROM professors;

title: Sum of all ECTS
description: What is the total number of ECTS credits across all subjects combined?
hint: Use SUM(ects) on the subjects table.
solution:
SELECT SUM(ects) AS total_ects
FROM subjects;

title: Average exam grade
description: What is the average grade across all exams? Round to 2 decimal places.
hint: Use ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)
solution:
SELECT ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM exams;

title: Min and max enrollment year
description: What are the earliest and latest student enrollment years in the database?
hint: Use MIN(enrollment_year) and MAX(enrollment_year) together.
solution:
SELECT
  MIN(enrollment_year) AS first_enrolled,
  MAX(enrollment_year) AS last_enrolled
FROM students;

title: Count distinct semesters
description: How many distinct semesters are there in the subjects table?
hint: Use COUNT(DISTINCT semester)
solution:
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT semester) AS num_semesters
FROM subjects;

title: Count passed exams
description: How many exams were passed (passed = true)?
hint: Use COUNT(*) with WHERE passed = true

title: Average grade for passing exams
description: What is the average grade for passed exams only? Round to 2 decimal places.
hint: Filter to passed = true before computing the average.

title: Total ECTS for first semester subjects
description: How many total ECTS credits do subjects taught in the first semester carry?
hint: Filter to semester = 1, then use SUM(ects).

title: Exam statistics in one query
description: Show the total number of exams, the average grade, and the minimum and maximum grade — all in one row.
hint: You can use multiple aggregate functions in a single SELECT.
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*)             AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
  MIN(grade)           AS min_grade,
  MAX(grade)           AS max_grade
FROM exams;

title: Count distinct students and subjects in exams
description: How many distinct students and how many distinct subjects appear in the exams table?
hint: Use COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) and COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id).
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS unique_students,
  COUNT(DISTINCT subject_id) AS unique_subjects
FROM exams;

title: Count excellent and perfect grades
description: Show the total number of exams, the number of grades >= 9, and the number of perfect grades (10). Use a FILTER clause.
hint: PostgreSQL supports COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE condition) for conditional aggregation.
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_exams,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE grade >= 9) AS excellent_count,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE grade = 10) AS perfect_count
FROM exams;

title: Total vs unique students in exams
description: Show the total number of rows in exams, the number of unique students, and the difference between the two.
hint: The difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) shows how many 'extra' exams exist in the system.
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*)                              AS total_entries,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id)            AS unique_students,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) AS extra_exams
FROM exams;

title: Calculate pass rate percentage
description: Calculate the exam pass rate. Show it as a decimal number from 0 to 100, rounded to 2 decimal places.
hint: Use FILTER(WHERE passed). Multiplying by 100.0 (not 100) ensures a decimal result.

title: Subjects per faculty with totals
description: For each faculty_id, show the number of subjects and the total number of ECTS credits. Sort by faculty_id.
hint: Group by faculty_id and use COUNT(*) and SUM(ects).
solution:
SELECT
  faculty_id,
  COUNT(*) AS num_subjects,
  SUM(ects) AS total_ects
FROM subjects
GROUP BY faculty_id
ORDER BY faculty_id;

title: Professors by title
description: Show how many professors hold each title. Sort by number of professors in descending order.
hint: GROUP BY title, COUNT(*)
solution:
SELECT
  title,
  COUNT(*) AS num_professors
FROM professors
GROUP BY title
ORDER BY num_professors DESC;

title: Average grade per student
description: For each student (student_id), show their average grade and total number of exams. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: GROUP BY student_id, use ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) and COUNT(*)
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
  COUNT(*) AS num_exams
FROM exams
GROUP BY student_id
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Students by enrollment year and faculty
description: Show the number of students grouped by enrollment year and faculty_id. Sort by year, then by faculty_id.
hint: GROUP BY enrollment_year, faculty_id
solution:
SELECT
  enrollment_year,
  faculty_id,
  COUNT(*) AS num_students
FROM students
GROUP BY enrollment_year, faculty_id
ORDER BY enrollment_year, faculty_id;

title: Subject statistics per semester
description: For each semester, show the number of subjects, the average ECTS, and the total ECTS. Sort by semester.
hint: GROUP BY semester, use COUNT(*), ROUND(AVG(ects), 2), and SUM(ects).
solution:
SELECT
  semester,
  COUNT(*)             AS num_subjects,
  ROUND(AVG(ects), 2) AS avg_ects,
  SUM(ects)            AS total_ects
FROM subjects
GROUP BY semester
ORDER BY semester;

title: Students with more than 1 exam
description: Show student_id and the number of exams for students who have taken more than 1 exam. Sort in descending order.
hint: GROUP BY student_id, then HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  COUNT(*) AS exam_count
FROM exams
GROUP BY student_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY exam_count DESC;

title: Professors teaching more than 3 subjects
description: Show professor_id and the number of subjects for professors who teach more than 3 subjects.
hint: GROUP BY professor_id from the professor_subjects table, HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
solution:
SELECT
  professor_id,
  COUNT(*) AS num_subjects
FROM professor_subjects
GROUP BY professor_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
ORDER BY num_subjects DESC;

title: Semesters with high total ECTS
description: Show the semester and total ECTS, but only for semesters where the total ECTS exceeds 40. Sort in descending order.
hint: GROUP BY semester, HAVING SUM(ects) > 40
solution:
SELECT
  semester,
  SUM(ects) AS total_ects
FROM subjects
GROUP BY semester
HAVING SUM(ects) > 40
ORDER BY total_ects DESC;

title: Faculties with more than 12 students
description: Show faculty_id values with more than 12 students, together with the number of students. Sort in descending order.
hint: GROUP BY faculty_id, HAVING COUNT(*) > 12
solution:
SELECT
  faculty_id,
  COUNT(*) AS num_students
FROM students
GROUP BY faculty_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 12
ORDER BY num_students DESC;

title: Semesters with multiple HAVING conditions
description: Show the semester, number of subjects, and total ECTS, but only for semesters that have at least 2 subjects AND a total ECTS greater than 10.
hint: HAVING can have multiple conditions joined with AND.
solution:
SELECT
  semester,
  COUNT(*) AS num_subjects,
  SUM(ects) AS total_ects
FROM subjects
GROUP BY semester
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
   AND SUM(ects) > 10
ORDER BY total_ects DESC;

title: Students with average grade above 8
description: Show student_id, average grade, and number of exams for students whose average grade is greater than 8. Sort in descending order.
hint: Use HAVING AVG(grade) > 8 — HAVING filters by the aggregate value, not by an individual row.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
  COUNT(*) AS num_exams
FROM exams
GROUP BY student_id
HAVING AVG(grade) > 8
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Faculty + year cohorts with enough students
description: For each combination of faculty_id and enrollment_year, show the number of students. Show only combinations with at least 3 students, sorted by number of students in descending order.
hint: GROUP BY two fields (faculty_id, enrollment_year), HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
solution:
SELECT
  faculty_id,
  enrollment_year,
  COUNT(*) AS student_count
FROM students
GROUP BY faculty_id, enrollment_year
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY student_count DESC, enrollment_year;

title: Pass rate per subject using FILTER
description: For each subject, show the number of attempts, the number of passed exams, the pass rate percentage, and the average grade. Sort by pass rate percentage in ascending order.
hint: Use COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE passed) for conditional aggregation within GROUP BY.
solution:
SELECT
  subject_id,
  COUNT(*) AS total_attempts,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE passed) AS passed_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE passed) / COUNT(*),
    1
  ) AS pass_rate_pct,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY subject_id
ORDER BY pass_rate_pct ASC, avg_grade ASC;

title: Departments with faculty names
description: Show the name of every department together with the name and city of the faculty it belongs to.
hint: Join departments and faculties using d.faculty_id = f.id
solution:
SELECT
  d.name AS department_name,
  f.name AS faculty_name,
  f.city
FROM departments d
INNER JOIN faculties f
  ON d.faculty_id = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, d.name;

title: Subjects with faculty names
description: Show the name of every subject, its number of ECTS credits, and the name of the faculty where it's taught.
hint: Join subjects and faculties using sub.faculty_id = f.id
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  f.name AS faculty_name
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN faculties f
  ON sub.faculty_id = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, sub.name;

title: Exams with student names
description: Show the student's first and last name, the exam date, and the grade for every exam. Sort by exam date in descending order.
hint: Join exams and students using e.student_id = s.id
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  e.exam_date,
  e.grade
FROM exams e
INNER JOIN students s
  ON e.student_id = s.id
ORDER BY e.exam_date DESC, s.last_name;

title: Exams with subject names
description: Show the subject name, grade, and exam date for every exam. Sort by subject name, then by grade in descending order.
hint: Join exams and subjects using e.subject_id = sub.id
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  e.grade,
  e.exam_date
FROM exams e
INNER JOIN subjects sub
  ON e.subject_id = sub.id
ORDER BY sub.name, e.grade DESC;

title: Students with faculty names
description: Show the student's first name, last name, and index number together with the name of the faculty they study at.
hint: Join students and faculties using s.faculty_id = f.id
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  s.index_number,
  f.name AS faculty_name
FROM students s
INNER JOIN faculties f
  ON s.faculty_id = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, s.last_name;

title: Students with department and faculty
description: For every student who has an assigned department, show their first name, last name, department name, and faculty name. Sort by faculty, then by department, then by last name.
hint: JOIN three tables: students → departments (department_id), students → faculties (faculty_id). Use WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL, or an INNER JOIN, which automatically excludes NULLs.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  d.name AS department_name,
  f.name AS faculty_name
FROM students s
INNER JOIN departments d ON s.department_id = d.id
INNER JOIN faculties f  ON s.faculty_id  = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, d.name, s.last_name;

title: Exams with subject and faculty
description: Show the faculty name, subject name, and grade for every exam. Sort by faculty name, then by subject.
hint: JOIN three tables: exams → subjects (subject_id) → faculties (faculty_id)
solution:
SELECT
  f.name AS faculty_name,
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  e.grade
FROM exams e
INNER JOIN subjects sub ON e.subject_id  = sub.id
INNER JOIN faculties f  ON sub.faculty_id = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, sub.name, e.grade DESC;

title: Professors with department and faculty
description: Show the professor's title, first name, and last name together with the department name and faculty name. Sort by faculty, then by department.
hint: JOIN three tables: professors → departments (department_id) → faculties (faculty_id). Departments are linked to faculties.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  d.name AS department_name,
  f.name AS faculty_name
FROM professors p
INNER JOIN departments d ON p.department_id = d.id
INNER JOIN faculties f  ON d.faculty_id   = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, d.name, p.last_name;

title: Subjects with optional department and faculty
description: Show the subject name, ECTS, faculty name, and department name (if it has one). Subjects with no department should appear with NULL in the department_name column.
hint: For faculty_id use an INNER JOIN (it can never be NULL), and for department_id use a LEFT JOIN since it's nullable.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  f.name  AS faculty_name,
  d.name  AS department_name
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN faculties f   ON sub.faculty_id    = f.id
LEFT  JOIN departments d ON sub.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY f.name, sub.name;

title: Exams with full context
description: For every exam, show the student's first and last name, the subject name, the first and last name of the professor who graded the exam, the date, and the grade. Sort by date in descending order.
hint: The exams table has student_id, subject_id, and professor_id — join all four tables. Use aliases for clarity.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name  || ' ' || s.last_name  AS student,
  sub.name                             AS subject_name,
  p.first_name  || ' ' || p.last_name  AS professor,
  e.exam_date,
  e.grade
FROM exams e
INNER JOIN students   s   ON e.student_id   = s.id
INNER JOIN subjects   sub ON e.subject_id   = sub.id
INNER JOIN professors p   ON e.professor_id = p.id
ORDER BY e.exam_date DESC, sub.name;

title: Professors and their assigned subjects
description: Show the title, first name, and last name of every professor together with the names of the subjects they teach (according to the professor_subjects table). Sort by the professor's last name, then by subject name.
hint: professor_subjects is a many-to-many junction table. Join: professors → professor_subjects → subjects.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sub.ects
FROM professors p
INNER JOIN professor_subjects ps ON p.id          = ps.professor_id
INNER JOIN subjects          sub ON ps.subject_id  = sub.id
ORDER BY p.last_name, sub.name;

title: Number of subjects per professor
description: For every professor, show their title, first name, last name, and the number of subjects they teach (from professor_subjects). Include professors who don't teach any subject. Sort by number of subjects in descending order.
hint: Use a LEFT JOIN to include professors with no subjects. COUNT(ps.subject_id) — not COUNT(*) — counts only matched rows.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  COUNT(ps.subject_id) AS num_subjects
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN professor_subjects ps ON p.id = ps.professor_id
GROUP BY p.id, p.title, p.first_name, p.last_name
ORDER BY num_subjects DESC, p.last_name;

title: Subjects taught by multiple professors
description: Show the subject name and the number of professors who teach it — but only for subjects taught by two or more professors. Sort by number of professors in descending order.
hint: JOIN subjects and professor_subjects, GROUP BY subject, HAVING COUNT(...) >= 2
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  COUNT(ps.professor_id) AS num_professors
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN professor_subjects ps ON sub.id = ps.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name, sub.ects
HAVING COUNT(ps.professor_id) >= 2
ORDER BY num_professors DESC, sub.name;

title: Professors without assigned subjects
description: Find professors who have no subject assigned in the professor_subjects table. Show their first name, last name, and title.
hint: LEFT JOIN professors with professor_subjects. Where there's no match, ps.professor_id will be NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  p.title
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN professor_subjects ps ON p.id = ps.professor_id
WHERE ps.professor_id IS NULL
ORDER BY p.last_name;

title: Subjects without assigned professors
description: Find subjects that have no professor in the professor_subjects table. Show the subject name, ECTS, and semester.
hint: LEFT JOIN subjects with professor_subjects. Where there's no match, ps.subject_id will be NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  sub.semester
FROM subjects sub
LEFT JOIN professor_subjects ps ON sub.id = ps.subject_id
WHERE ps.subject_id IS NULL
ORDER BY sub.name;

title: Average grade per professor
description: For every professor who has graded at least one exam, show their first name, last name, title, number of exams, and average grade. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: The exams table has professor_id. JOIN professors and exams, GROUP BY professor, ROUND(AVG(grade), 2).
solution:
SELECT
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  p.title,
  COUNT(*) AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM professors p
INNER JOIN exams e ON p.id = e.professor_id
GROUP BY p.id, p.first_name, p.last_name, p.title
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Subjects never taken as exam
description: Show all subjects that have never been the subject of an exam — they don't appear in any row of the exams table.
hint: Use WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT subject_id FROM exams). Note: if the query returns 0 rows, that means every subject in the database has been used in at least one exam — that is the correct result.
solution:
SELECT id, name, ects, semester
FROM subjects
WHERE id NOT IN (
  SELECT DISTINCT subject_id
  FROM exams
)
ORDER BY name;

title: Students who have taken at least one exam
description: Show the first name, last name, and index number of students who have taken at least one exam.
hint: Use WHERE id IN (SELECT DISTINCT student_id FROM exams)
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, index_number
FROM students
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT DISTINCT student_id
  FROM exams
)
ORDER BY last_name, first_name;

title: Students who have never taken an exam
description: Show the first name, last name, index number, and enrollment year of students who have NEVER taken an exam.
hint: Use WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT student_id FROM exams)
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, index_number, enrollment_year
FROM students
WHERE id NOT IN (
  SELECT DISTINCT student_id
  FROM exams
)
ORDER BY last_name;

title: Faculties with at least one department
description: Show the name and city of every faculty that has at least one department in the departments table.
hint: Use WHERE id IN (SELECT DISTINCT faculty_id FROM departments)
solution:
SELECT name, city
FROM faculties
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT DISTINCT faculty_id
  FROM departments
)
ORDER BY name;

title: Faculty with the most students
description: Show the name and city of the faculty with the largest number of students. Use a scalar subquery.
hint: The scalar subquery returns a single faculty_id: SELECT faculty_id FROM students GROUP BY faculty_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC LIMIT 1
solution:
SELECT name, city
FROM faculties
WHERE id = (
  SELECT faculty_id
  FROM students
  GROUP BY faculty_id
  ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC
  LIMIT 1
);

title: Students with any grade above overall average
description: Show the first name, last name, and index number of students who have at least one grade higher than the average of all exams.
hint: Inner query: SELECT student_id FROM exams WHERE grade > (SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams). Outer query: WHERE id IN (...)

title: Subjects with above-average exam grade
description: Show the name of subjects whose average exam grade is higher than the overall average across all exams.
hint: The inner query groups exams by subject_id and uses HAVING AVG(grade) > (SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams). The outer query fetches the names.
solution:
SELECT name
FROM subjects
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT subject_id
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY subject_id
  HAVING AVG(grade) > (
    SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams
  )
)
ORDER BY name;

title: Professor(s) with the most assigned subjects
description: Show the first name, last name, and title of the professor(s) who teach the largest number of subjects (there may be more than one professor tied for the maximum).
hint: The inner subquery finds the MAX number of subjects: SELECT MAX(cnt) FROM (SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM professor_subjects GROUP BY professor_id) t. The outer query filters for professors who reach that max.
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, title
FROM professors
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT professor_id
  FROM professor_subjects
  GROUP BY professor_id
  HAVING COUNT(*) = (
    SELECT MAX(cnt)
    FROM (
      SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt
      FROM professor_subjects
      GROUP BY professor_id
    ) t
  )
)
ORDER BY last_name;

title: Subjects with more exams than average
description: Show the name of subjects that had more exams than the average number of exams per subject.
hint: The inner query computes the average: SELECT AVG(exam_count) FROM (SELECT subject_id, COUNT(*) AS exam_count FROM exams GROUP BY subject_id) t. The outer query filters for subjects that exceed that average.
solution:
SELECT name
FROM subjects
WHERE id IN (
  SELECT subject_id
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY subject_id
  HAVING COUNT(*) > (
    SELECT AVG(exam_count)
    FROM (
      SELECT subject_id, COUNT(*) AS exam_count
      FROM exams
      GROUP BY subject_id
    ) t
  )
)
ORDER BY name;

title: Students above their department average
description: Show the first name, last name, and department_id of students whose own average grade is higher than the average of all students in the same department. Include only students who have a department.
hint: Correlated subquery: for each student in the outer query, the inner query computes the average for their department. The correlation: WHERE s2.department_id = s.department_id
solution:
SELECT s.first_name, s.last_name, s.department_id
FROM students s
WHERE s.department_id IS NOT NULL
  AND (
    SELECT AVG(e.grade)
    FROM exams e
    WHERE e.student_id = s.id
  ) > (
    SELECT AVG(e2.grade)
    FROM exams e2
    INNER JOIN students s2 ON e2.student_id = s2.id
    WHERE s2.department_id = s.department_id
  )
ORDER BY s.last_name;

title: Exams above subject average grade
description: Show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and that subject's average grade, for every exam where the grade is higher than the average grade of the same subject.
hint: Correlated subquery in the WHERE clause: grade > (SELECT AVG(e2.grade) FROM exams e2 WHERE e2.subject_id = e.subject_id). You can also add a computed column with the average using the same subquery in the SELECT.
solution:
SELECT
  e.student_id,
  e.subject_id,
  e.grade,
  ROUND((
    SELECT AVG(e2.grade)
    FROM exams e2
    WHERE e2.subject_id = e.subject_id
  ), 2) AS subject_avg
FROM exams e
WHERE e.grade > (
  SELECT AVG(e2.grade)
  FROM exams e2
  WHERE e2.subject_id = e.subject_id
)
ORDER BY e.subject_id, e.grade DESC;

title: Professors who have graded at least one exam
description: Using EXISTS, find professors who have graded at least one exam in the exams table.
hint: Correlated EXISTS: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM exams e WHERE e.professor_id = p.id)
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, title
FROM professors p
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM exams e
  WHERE e.professor_id = p.id
)
ORDER BY last_name;

title: Departments with above-average student grades
description: Show the name of departments whose students have an average exam grade higher than the overall average across all exams.
hint: The inner query groups students by department_id and uses HAVING AVG(e.grade) > (SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams). The outer query fetches the department names.
solution:
SELECT d.name AS department_name
FROM departments d
WHERE d.id IN (
  SELECT s.department_id
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  GROUP BY s.department_id
  HAVING AVG(e.grade) > (
    SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams
  )
)
ORDER BY d.name;

title: Basic passed/failed status
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and a new column passed_status with the values 'Passed' or 'Failed'. Sort by student_id, then by subject_id.
hint: Use CASE WHEN passed THEN 'Passed' ELSE 'Failed' END AS passed_status
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  CASE WHEN passed THEN 'Passed' ELSE 'Failed' END AS passed_status
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, subject_id;

title: Grade classification label
description: For each exam, show the grade and a label: grade 10 = 'Excellent (10)', grades 8-9 = 'Very good', grades 6-7 = 'Passed', grade 5 = 'Failed'. Sort by grade in descending order.
hint: CASE WHEN evaluates conditions in order — put the most specific condition first. Use grade = 10, grade >= 8, grade >= 6, and ELSE for everything else.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  CASE
    WHEN grade = 10 THEN 'Excellent (10)'
    WHEN grade >= 8  THEN 'Very good'
    WHEN grade >= 6  THEN 'Passed'
    ELSE                  'Failed'
  END AS grade_label
FROM exams
ORDER BY grade DESC;

title: ECTS difficulty category
description: For each subject, show the name, the number of ECTS credits, and a category: ects >= 8 = 'Demanding', ects >= 5 = 'Medium', everything else = 'Easy'. Sort by ECTS in descending order.
hint: CASE WHEN ects >= 8 THEN ... WHEN ects >= 5 THEN ... ELSE ... END AS ects_category
solution:
SELECT
  name,
  ects,
  CASE
    WHEN ects >= 8 THEN 'Demanding'
    WHEN ects >= 5 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE                'Easy'
  END AS ects_category
FROM subjects
ORDER BY ects DESC, name;

title: Enrollment year label
description: For each student, show the first name, last name, enrollment year, and a generation label: 2023 = 'First year', 2022 = 'Second year', 2021 = 'Third year', everything else = 'Older generation'. Sort by enrollment year in descending order, then by last name.
hint: Use CASE enrollment_year WHEN 2023 THEN ... WHEN 2022 THEN ... END for the simple CASE variant.
solution:
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  enrollment_year,
  CASE enrollment_year
    WHEN 2023 THEN 'First year'
    WHEN 2022 THEN 'Second year'
    WHEN 2021 THEN 'Third year'
    ELSE           'Older generation'
  END AS year_label
FROM students
ORDER BY enrollment_year DESC, last_name;

title: Passed and failed count per subject
description: For each subject (subject_id), show the total number of exams, the number passed, and the number failed. Use CASE WHEN inside COUNT. Sort by subject_id.
hint: COUNT(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 END) only counts rows where the condition is true — NULL values aren't counted. Alternative: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE passed).
solution:
SELECT
  subject_id,
  COUNT(*)                              AS total,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 END)    AS passed_count,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN NOT passed THEN 1 END) AS failed_count
FROM exams
GROUP BY subject_id
ORDER BY subject_id;

title: ECTS sum by category per faculty
description: For each faculty (faculty_id), show the sum of ECTS credits for demanding subjects (ects >= 8), medium ones (5-7), and easy ones (up to 4). Sort by faculty_id.
hint: Use SUM(CASE WHEN ects >= 8 THEN ects ELSE 0 END) for conditional summing within GROUP BY.
solution:
SELECT
  faculty_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN ects >= 8            THEN ects ELSE 0 END) AS high_ects,
  SUM(CASE WHEN ects BETWEEN 5 AND 7 THEN ects ELSE 0 END) AS medium_ects,
  SUM(CASE WHEN ects <= 4            THEN ects ELSE 0 END) AS low_ects
FROM subjects
GROUP BY faculty_id
ORDER BY faculty_id;

title: Pass rate per subject with CASE WHEN
description: For each subject, show subject_id, the total number of exams, and the pass rate rounded to one decimal place. Use SUM with CASE WHEN. Sort by pass rate in descending order.
hint: SUM(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*) * 100.0 gives the percentage. Multiply by 100.0 (not 100) to get a decimal result.
solution:
SELECT
  subject_id,
  COUNT(*) AS total_attempts,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*),
    1
  ) AS pass_rate_pct
FROM exams
GROUP BY subject_id
ORDER BY pass_rate_pct DESC;

title: Professor title group
description: For each professor, show the first name, last name, title, and group: professors whose title contains 'profesor' = 'Professor', docents = 'Docent', everyone else = 'Associate'. Sort by group, then by last name.
hint: Use CASE WHEN title LIKE '%profesor%' THEN ... WHEN title = 'docent' THEN ... ELSE ... END
solution:
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  title,
  CASE
    WHEN title LIKE '%profesor%' THEN 'Professor'
    WHEN title = 'docent'        THEN 'Docent'
    ELSE                              'Associate'
  END AS title_group
FROM professors
ORDER BY title_group, last_name;

title: Classify subjects by average exam grade
description: For each subject that has been examined, show the name, the average grade, and a classification: avg >= 8 = 'Easy subject', avg >= 6.5 = 'Medium', below that = 'Difficult subject'. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: JOIN subjects and exams, GROUP BY subject, then CASE WHEN AVG(grade) >= 8 ... in the SELECT. AVG is computed per group, so it can be used inside CASE.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
  CASE
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 8.0 THEN 'Easy subject'
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 6.5 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE                          'Difficult subject'
  END AS difficulty_label
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Classify students by average grade
description: For each student who has at least one exam, show the first name, last name, average grade, and performance: avg >= 9 = 'Excellent', avg >= 7.5 = 'Very good', avg >= 6.5 = 'Good', below that = 'Sufficient'. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: JOIN students and exams, GROUP BY student, then CASE WHEN in the SELECT over AVG(grade).
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
  CASE
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 9.0 THEN 'Excellent'
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 7.5 THEN 'Very good'
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 6.5 THEN 'Good'
    ELSE                          'Sufficient'
  END AS performance
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Faculty size classification
description: For each faculty, show the name, the number of students, and a size label: >= 20 = 'Large', >= 10 = 'Medium', below that = 'Small'. Include faculties with no students too. Sort by number of students in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN faculties with students, GROUP BY faculty, then CASE WHEN COUNT(s.id) >= 20 ... Use COUNT(s.id) so NULL rows from the LEFT JOIN are counted correctly.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name AS faculty_name,
  COUNT(s.id) AS student_count,
  CASE
    WHEN COUNT(s.id) >= 20 THEN 'Large'
    WHEN COUNT(s.id) >= 10 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE                        'Small'
  END AS size_label
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN students s ON f.id = s.faculty_id
GROUP BY f.id, f.name
ORDER BY student_count DESC;

title: Student performance vs overall average
description: For each student with exams, show the first name, last name, their own average grade, the overall average across all exams, and a comparison: 'Above average', 'At average', or 'Below average'. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: A scalar subquery (SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams) can be used both in SELECT and in CASE WHEN. Combine GROUP BY with CASE over an aggregate value.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2)                   AS student_avg,
  ROUND((SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams), 2) AS overall_avg,
  CASE
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) > (SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams) THEN 'Above average'
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) = (SELECT AVG(grade) FROM exams) THEN 'At average'
    ELSE                                                     'Below average'
  END AS comparison
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name
ORDER BY student_avg DESC;

title: UNION: unique first names
description: Show all unique first_name values that appear in the students table OR the professors table. Sort alphabetically.
hint: UNION automatically removes duplicates. Both SELECTs must return the same number of columns of the same type.
solution:
SELECT first_name FROM students
UNION
SELECT first_name FROM professors
ORDER BY first_name;

title: UNION ALL: all first names with duplicates
description: Show ALL first names (first_name) from the students table and the professors table — without removing duplicates. Sort alphabetically. Compare the row count with the previous UNION task.
hint: UNION ALL does not remove duplicates — it's faster than UNION. The result can contain the same name more than once if it appears in both tables.
solution:
SELECT first_name FROM students
UNION ALL
SELECT first_name FROM professors
ORDER BY first_name;

title: UNION: department IDs from students and professors
description: Show all unique department_id values used by students or professors. Exclude NULL values. Sort in ascending order.
hint: Filter out NULL in both SELECTs with WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL. UNION removes duplicates.
solution:
SELECT department_id FROM students   WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
UNION
SELECT department_id FROM professors WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY department_id;

title: INTERSECT: shared department IDs
description: Find department_id values that appear for BOTH STUDENTS AND PROFESSORS — departments that have both students and teaching staff. Sort in ascending order.
hint: INTERSECT returns only rows that exist in BOTH results. Filter out NULL in both queries.
solution:
SELECT department_id FROM students   WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
INTERSECT
SELECT department_id FROM professors WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY department_id;

title: EXCEPT: professors-only department IDs
description: Find department_id values used by professors but not by students — departments with teaching staff only, no students. Sort in ascending order.
hint: EXCEPT returns rows that are in the first set but NOT in the second. Query order matters: A EXCEPT B gives 'in A but not in B'.
solution:
SELECT department_id FROM professors WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
EXCEPT
SELECT department_id FROM students   WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY department_id;

title: INTERSECT: subjects in both exams and professor_subjects
description: Find subject_id values that appear in BOTH the exams table and the professor_subjects table — subjects that have been examined and are also assigned to professors. Sort in ascending order.
hint: INTERSECT operates on sets of rows. Here it compares the sets of subject_id values from two different tables.
solution:
SELECT subject_id FROM exams
INTERSECT
SELECT subject_id FROM professor_subjects
ORDER BY subject_id;

title: EXCEPT: subjects assigned to professors but never examined
description: Find subject_id values that exist in professor_subjects but are NOT in any exam — subjects assigned to professors that haven't been examined yet.
hint: A EXCEPT B: the subject_ids that are in professor_subjects but not in exams. The result can be empty if every subject has been examined.
solution:
SELECT subject_id FROM professor_subjects
EXCEPT
SELECT subject_id FROM exams
ORDER BY subject_id;

title: UNION ALL with source type column
description: Build a combined list of every person in the database: show first_name, last_name, and a source label ('Student' or 'Professor'). Include everyone — duplicates too. Sort by last name, then by first name.
hint: Add a literal as a third column: SELECT first_name, last_name, 'Student' AS person_type ... UNION ALL SELECT first_name, last_name, 'Professor' ...
solution:
SELECT first_name, last_name, 'Student'   AS person_type FROM students
UNION ALL
SELECT first_name, last_name, 'Professor' AS person_type FROM professors
ORDER BY last_name, first_name, person_type;

title: EXCEPT: students-only department IDs with ORDER BY
description: Find department_id values used by students but NOT by professors. Sort in descending order. (If the result is empty, it means professors cover every department that has students.)
hint: ORDER BY in set operations goes at the end of the whole query and applies to the combined result.
solution:
SELECT department_id FROM students   WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
EXCEPT
SELECT department_id FROM professors WHERE department_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY department_id DESC;

title: UNION: students with grade 10 and students without any exam
description: Build a list of student IDs who received a grade of 10 on at least one exam (category 'Excellent'), and the IDs of students who haven't taken any exam at all (category 'No exams'). Sort by category, then by student_id.
hint: First branch: SELECT DISTINCT student_id, 'Excellent' FROM exams WHERE grade = 10. Second branch: SELECT id, 'No exams' FROM students WHERE id NOT IN (...). UNION combines them.
solution:
SELECT DISTINCT student_id AS id, 'Excellent' AS category
FROM exams
WHERE grade = 10
UNION
SELECT id, 'No exams' AS category
FROM students
WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT student_id FROM exams)
ORDER BY category, id;

title: Basic CTE: filter passed exams
description: Using WITH, define a CTE named passed_exams that contains only passed exams (passed = true). Then, from that CTE, show all rows sorted by grade in descending order.
hint: Syntax: WITH name AS (SELECT ...) SELECT ... FROM name ...
solution:
WITH passed_exams AS (
  SELECT student_id, subject_id, grade, exam_date
  FROM exams
  WHERE passed = true
)
SELECT *
FROM passed_exams
ORDER BY grade DESC;

title: CTE: average grade per student
description: Define a CTE student_averages that computes the average grade and number of exams for each student. Then join that CTE with the students table to get the first name, last name, average, and exam count. Sort by average in descending order.
hint: The CTE groups exams by student_id. The outer SELECT joins the CTE with the students table.
solution:
WITH student_averages AS (
  SELECT
    student_id,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
    COUNT(*)             AS exam_count
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY student_id
)
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  sa.avg_grade,
  sa.exam_count
FROM student_averages sa
INNER JOIN students s ON sa.student_id = s.id
ORDER BY sa.avg_grade DESC;

title: CTE: exam stats per subject
description: Define a CTE exam_stats that computes the number of exams and the average grade for each subject. Join it with subjects to get the subject name. Show only subjects with at least 3 exams. Sort by average in descending order.
hint: A CTE can contain GROUP BY and aggregates. The outer SELECT filters on columns from the CTE.
solution:
WITH exam_stats AS (
  SELECT
    subject_id,
    COUNT(*)             AS total_exams,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY subject_id
)
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  es.total_exams,
  es.avg_grade
FROM exam_stats es
INNER JOIN subjects sub ON es.subject_id = sub.id
WHERE es.total_exams >= 3
ORDER BY es.avg_grade DESC;

title: CTE: department info with faculty
description: Define a CTE dept_info that joins departments and faculties (department name + faculty name and city). Then, from that CTE, show students together with their department and faculty. Sort by faculty name, department name, and last name.
hint: A CTE can contain a JOIN. The outer SELECT then joins the CTE with students instead of repeating the same JOIN.
solution:
WITH dept_info AS (
  SELECT
    d.id          AS dept_id,
    d.name        AS dept_name,
    f.name        AS faculty_name,
    f.city
  FROM departments d
  INNER JOIN faculties f ON d.faculty_id = f.id
)
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  di.dept_name,
  di.faculty_name
FROM students s
INNER JOIN dept_info di ON s.department_id = di.dept_id
ORDER BY di.faculty_name, di.dept_name, s.last_name;

title: Two CTEs: passed count and average grade
description: Define two CTEs: passed_stats (number of passed exams per student) and avg_stats (average grade per student). Join both CTEs with students and show the first name, last name, number passed, and average. Sort by average in descending order.
hint: Multiple CTEs are separated by a comma: WITH cte1 AS (...), cte2 AS (...) SELECT ...
solution:
WITH passed_stats AS (
  SELECT student_id, COUNT(*) AS passed_count
  FROM exams
  WHERE passed = true
  GROUP BY student_id
),
avg_stats AS (
  SELECT student_id, ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY student_id
)
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  ps.passed_count,
  av.avg_grade
FROM students s
INNER JOIN passed_stats ps ON s.id = ps.student_id
INNER JOIN avg_stats    av ON s.id = av.student_id
ORDER BY av.avg_grade DESC;

title: CTE: subject count per professor
description: Define a CTE prof_load that computes the number of subjects per professor (from professor_subjects). Join it with professors and show the first name, last name, title, and number of subjects. Sort by number of subjects in descending order.
hint: The CTE encapsulates the counting logic. The outer SELECT joins the CTE with professors for a readable final query.
solution:
WITH prof_load AS (
  SELECT professor_id, COUNT(*) AS subject_count
  FROM professor_subjects
  GROUP BY professor_id
)
SELECT
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  p.title,
  pl.subject_count
FROM professors p
INNER JOIN prof_load pl ON p.id = pl.professor_id
ORDER BY pl.subject_count DESC, p.last_name;

title: CTE: subjects with below-average passing rate
description: Define a CTE subject_pass_rate that computes the pass rate for each subject. Show only subjects whose pass rate is below 70%. Sort by percentage in ascending order.
hint: The CTE computes pass_rate_pct. The outer SELECT filters WHERE pass_rate_pct < 70 and joins with subjects for the name.
solution:
WITH subject_pass_rate AS (
  SELECT
    subject_id,
    ROUND(
      100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*),
      1
    ) AS pass_rate_pct
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY subject_id
)
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  spr.pass_rate_pct
FROM subject_pass_rate spr
INNER JOIN subjects sub ON spr.subject_id = sub.id
WHERE spr.pass_rate_pct < 70
ORDER BY spr.pass_rate_pct ASC;

title: CTE with HAVING: high-performing departments
description: Define a CTE dept_grades that computes the average student grade per department (joining students → exams). Show only departments where the average is above 7.0. Join with departments for the names. Sort by average in descending order.
hint: A CTE can have a HAVING clause to filter aggregate results. The outer SELECT joins the CTE result with departments. Threshold: HAVING AVG(e.grade) > 7.0
solution:
WITH dept_grades AS (
  SELECT
    s.department_id,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  WHERE s.department_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY s.department_id
  HAVING AVG(e.grade) > 7.0
)
SELECT
  d.name AS department_name,
  dg.avg_grade
FROM dept_grades dg
INNER JOIN departments d ON dg.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY dg.avg_grade DESC;

title: Two CTEs: faculty student count and exam stats
description: Define two CTEs: faculty_students (number of students per faculty) and faculty_exams (average grade and number of exams per faculty, via students). Join both with faculties and show a complete overview. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: The second CTE can use a JOIN inside itself. faculty_exams needs a students → exams JOIN, then GROUP BY faculty_id.
solution:
WITH faculty_students AS (
  SELECT faculty_id, COUNT(*) AS student_count
  FROM students
  GROUP BY faculty_id
),
faculty_exams AS (
  SELECT
    s.faculty_id,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
    COUNT(*)               AS total_exams
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  GROUP BY s.faculty_id
)
SELECT
  f.name         AS faculty_name,
  fs.student_count,
  fe.avg_grade,
  fe.total_exams
FROM faculties f
INNER JOIN faculty_students fs ON f.id = fs.faculty_id
LEFT  JOIN faculty_exams    fe ON f.id = fe.faculty_id
ORDER BY fe.avg_grade DESC NULLS LAST;

title: Three CTEs: overall avg → student avg → above average
description: Define three CTEs: overall_avg (the overall average across all exams), student_avg (average per student), above_avg (students above the overall average). The final SELECT shows the first name, last name, and average of students above the overall average. Sort in descending order.
hint: A later CTE can reference an earlier one: above_avg can use student_avg and overall_avg in its WHERE clause.
solution:
WITH overall_avg AS (
  SELECT AVG(grade) AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
),
student_avg AS (
  SELECT student_id, AVG(grade) AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY student_id
),
above_avg AS (
  SELECT sa.student_id, ROUND(sa.avg_grade, 2) AS student_avg
  FROM student_avg sa, overall_avg oa
  WHERE sa.avg_grade > oa.avg_grade
)
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  aa.student_avg
FROM above_avg aa
INNER JOIN students s ON aa.student_id = s.id
ORDER BY aa.student_avg DESC;

title: CTE: top student per faculty
description: Using CTEs, find the student with the highest average grade per faculty. Show the first name, last name, faculty name, and average grade. (If several students share the maximum, show all of them.)
hint: The student_avg CTE computes the average per student. The faculty_best CTE takes MAX(avg_grade) per faculty_id. The final SELECT joins them to filter students at the maximum.
solution:
WITH student_avg AS (
  SELECT
    s.id         AS student_id,
    s.first_name,
    s.last_name,
    s.faculty_id,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name, s.faculty_id
),
faculty_best AS (
  SELECT faculty_id, MAX(avg_grade) AS best_avg
  FROM student_avg
  GROUP BY faculty_id
)
SELECT
  sa.first_name,
  sa.last_name,
  f.name AS faculty_name,
  sa.avg_grade
FROM student_avg sa
INNER JOIN faculty_best fb
  ON sa.faculty_id = fb.faculty_id
  AND sa.avg_grade = fb.best_avg
INNER JOIN faculties f ON sa.faculty_id = f.id
ORDER BY f.name, sa.last_name;

title: Multi-CTE: exam stats with difficulty label
description: Define two CTEs: exam_stats (number of exams, number passed, average grade per subject) and subject_difficulty (a CASE WHEN classification based on the average: Easy/Medium/Hard). The final SELECT shows the subject name, the stats, and the classification. Sort by average.
hint: The second CTE subject_difficulty references the first CTE exam_stats. It uses CASE WHEN over the avg_grade column from the first CTE.
solution:
WITH exam_stats AS (
  SELECT
    subject_id,
    COUNT(*)                              AS total_attempts,
    COUNT(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 END)    AS passed_count,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)                  AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY subject_id
),
subject_difficulty AS (
  SELECT
    subject_id,
    avg_grade,
    CASE
      WHEN avg_grade >= 8.0 THEN 'Easy'
      WHEN avg_grade >= 6.5 THEN 'Medium'
      ELSE                       'Hard'
    END AS difficulty_label
  FROM exam_stats
)
SELECT
  sub.name           AS subject_name,
  es.total_attempts,
  es.passed_count,
  es.avg_grade,
  sd.difficulty_label
FROM exam_stats es
INNER JOIN subject_difficulty sd ON es.subject_id = sd.subject_id
INNER JOIN subjects           sub ON es.subject_id = sub.id
ORDER BY es.avg_grade ASC;

title: Overall average alongside each exam
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and the overall average grade across all exams in the same row. Use AVG() OVER() without PARTITION BY.
hint: AVG(grade) OVER() — an empty OVER() clause means: a single window that spans all rows.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  ROUND(AVG(grade) OVER(), 2) AS overall_avg
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, subject_id;

title: Subject average alongside each exam
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and the average grade for that subject. Use AVG() OVER(PARTITION BY subject_id).
hint: PARTITION BY subject_id splits the rows into groups by subject. The average is computed within each group, but the rows aren't aggregated — the row count stays the same.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  ROUND(AVG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY subject_id), 2) AS subject_avg
FROM exams
ORDER BY subject_id, grade DESC;

title: Exam count per student on each row
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and that student's total exam count — without GROUP BY, on every row.
hint: COUNT(*) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id) — counts all rows within the same student_id.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  COUNT(*) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id) AS student_exam_count
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, subject_id;

title: Student average alongside each exam
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and that student's average grade. Use AVG() OVER(PARTITION BY student_id) to get the average without GROUP BY.
hint: PARTITION BY student_id splits the window by student. Each row keeps its own student's average.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  ROUND(AVG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id), 2) AS student_avg
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, subject_id;

title: Rank exams by grade
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and the grade's rank among all exams — from highest to lowest. Exams with the same grade get the same rank (with a gap before the next rank).
hint: RANK() OVER(ORDER BY grade DESC) — the same rank for equal grades, then a gap (e.g. 1,1,3). Sort the result by grade_rank.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  RANK() OVER(ORDER BY grade DESC) AS grade_rank
FROM exams
ORDER BY grade_rank, student_id;

title: RANK vs DENSE_RANK: spot the difference
description: For the first 20 exams sorted by grade in descending order, show the grade, RANK, and DENSE_RANK. Notice the difference: RANK skips numbers after a tie, DENSE_RANK does not.
hint: RANK(): 1,1,3,4... — skips 2. DENSE_RANK(): 1,1,2,3... — no skipping. Both use the same OVER(ORDER BY grade DESC).
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  RANK()       OVER(ORDER BY grade DESC) AS rnk,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER(ORDER BY grade DESC) AS dense_rnk
FROM exams
ORDER BY grade DESC, student_id
LIMIT 20;

title: Row number per student (exam sequence)
description: For each exam, show student_id, subject_id, the exam date, the grade, and that exam's sequence number within the student — ordered by exam date. Use ROW_NUMBER().
hint: ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) — each student gets their own sequence 1, 2, 3...
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) AS exam_seq
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_seq;

title: Rank students by average grade
description: Compute the average grade per student and rank the students from best to worst. Show the first name, last name, average, and rank. Use a CTE for the aggregation, then RANK() for the ranking.
hint: The most common pattern: a CTE aggregates (GROUP BY), the outer SELECT applies RANK() OVER(ORDER BY avg_grade DESC) to that result.
solution:
WITH student_avg AS (
  SELECT
    student_id,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
    COUNT(*)             AS exam_count
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY student_id
)
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  sa.avg_grade,
  sa.exam_count,
  RANK() OVER(ORDER BY sa.avg_grade DESC) AS grade_rank
FROM student_avg sa
INNER JOIN students s ON sa.student_id = s.id
ORDER BY grade_rank, s.last_name;

title: Rank subjects by average grade
description: Compute the average grade and number of attempts per subject, then rank the subjects from easiest (high average) to hardest. Show the subject name, average, number of attempts, and rank.
hint: A CTE computes the average and count per subject_id. The outer SELECT joins with subjects and applies RANK() OVER(ORDER BY avg_grade DESC).
solution:
WITH subject_avg AS (
  SELECT
    subject_id,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
    COUNT(*)             AS attempts
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY subject_id
)
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sa.avg_grade,
  sa.attempts,
  RANK() OVER(ORDER BY sa.avg_grade DESC) AS easiness_rank
FROM subject_avg sa
INNER JOIN subjects sub ON sa.subject_id = sub.id
ORDER BY easiness_rank;

title: Rank students within their department
description: For each student with a department, compute the average grade and rank them within their department, sorted from best to worst. Show the first name, last name, department_id, average, and rank within the department.
hint: PARTITION BY department_id inside RANK() OVER(...) means: independent ranking within each department. Use a CTE for the averages, then RANK() with PARTITION BY.
solution:
WITH student_avg AS (
  SELECT
    s.id           AS student_id,
    s.first_name,
    s.last_name,
    s.department_id,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  WHERE s.department_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name, s.department_id
)
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  department_id,
  avg_grade,
  RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY avg_grade DESC) AS dept_rank
FROM student_avg
ORDER BY department_id, dept_rank;

title: Previous exam grade per student (LAG)
description: For each exam, show student_id, date, grade, and the grade from that same student's previous exam (sorted by date). For the first exam, the previous grade should be NULL.
hint: LAG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) returns the value from the previous row within the partition. For the first row, there's no previous one — the result is NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  LAG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) AS prev_grade
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_date;

title: Next exam grade per student (LEAD)
description: For each exam, show student_id, date, grade, and the grade from that same student's next exam. For the last exam, the next grade should be NULL.
hint: LEAD(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) returns the value from the next row. For the last row in the partition, the result is NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  LEAD(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) AS next_grade
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_date;

title: Grade difference from previous exam
description: For each exam, show student_id, date, grade, the previous grade, and the difference (grade - prev_grade). A positive difference means improvement, negative means a drop. For the first exam, the difference is NULL.
hint: Compute LAG(grade) OVER(...) and subtract it from grade. You can use the same window function twice, or a WINDOW clause for readability.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  LAG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date)             AS prev_grade,
  grade - LAG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date)    AS grade_diff
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_date;

title: Running exam count per student
description: For each exam, show student_id, date, grade, and that student's cumulative exam count up to that point (including the current one). Use COUNT() OVER() with ORDER BY.
hint: COUNT(*) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) — when ORDER BY is present, the default frame is RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW, which gives a cumulative count.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  COUNT(*) OVER(
    PARTITION BY student_id
    ORDER BY exam_date
  ) AS running_exam_count
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_date;

title: Running average grade per student
description: For each exam, show student_id, date, grade, and that student's cumulative average grade up to and including that exam. Use AVG() OVER() with an explicit ROWS frame.
hint: AVG(grade) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) — an explicit ROWS frame makes the intent clear.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  ROUND(
    AVG(grade) OVER(
      PARTITION BY student_id
      ORDER BY exam_date
      ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ), 2
  ) AS running_avg
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_date;

title: Filter exams by year
description: Show all exams that took place in the year 2023. Show student_id, subject_id, the grade, and the date. Sort by date.
hint: Use EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date) = 2023 in the WHERE clause.
solution:
SELECT student_id, subject_id, grade, exam_date
FROM exams
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date) = 2023
ORDER BY exam_date;

title: Extract year and month from exam date
description: For each exam, show the grade, the date, and the year and month of the exam as separate numeric columns. Sort by date.
hint: EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date) AS exam_year, EXTRACT(MONTH FROM exam_date) AS exam_month — EXTRACT returns a numeric value.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  exam_date,
  EXTRACT(YEAR  FROM exam_date)::INT AS exam_year,
  EXTRACT(MONTH FROM exam_date)::INT AS exam_month
FROM exams
ORDER BY exam_date;

title: Earliest and latest exam dates
description: Show the earliest exam date, the latest exam date, and the difference between them in days.
hint: MIN(exam_date) and MAX(exam_date) are aggregate functions. Subtracting two DATE values in PostgreSQL returns an INTEGER — the number of days.
solution:
SELECT
  MIN(exam_date)                  AS earliest_exam,
  MAX(exam_date)                  AS latest_exam,
  MAX(exam_date) - MIN(exam_date) AS days_span
FROM exams;

title: Exam count and average grade per year
description: For each year in which exams took place, show the year, the number of exams, and the average grade. Sort by year.
hint: GROUP BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date). EXTRACT returns a float8 — you can cast it to INT with ::INT.
solution:
SELECT
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date)::INT AS exam_year,
  COUNT(*)                          AS exam_count,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)              AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date)
ORDER BY exam_year;

title: Exam count and average grade per month
description: For each month that appears in the data, show the month number, the number of exams, and the average grade. Sort by month.
hint: GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH FROM exam_date). The same month from different years is grouped together.
solution:
SELECT
  EXTRACT(MONTH FROM exam_date)::INT AS exam_month,
  COUNT(*)                           AS exam_count,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)               AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH FROM exam_date)
ORDER BY exam_month;

title: Format exam date with TO_CHAR
description: For the first 10 exams (sorted by date), show the date in three formats: 'DD.MM.YYYY.', 'Month YYYY', and the day of the year ('DDD'). Use TO_CHAR.
hint: TO_CHAR(exam_date, 'format_string') converts a date to text according to the given format. Each format uses a different format string.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  exam_date,
  TO_CHAR(exam_date, 'DD.MM.YYYY.') AS date_dmy,
  TO_CHAR(exam_date, 'Month YYYY')  AS month_year,
  TO_CHAR(exam_date, 'DDD')         AS day_of_year
FROM exams
ORDER BY exam_date
LIMIT 10;

title: Group exams by month using DATE_TRUNC
description: Using DATE_TRUNC, group exams by year and month. Show the month as the date of the first day of that month, the number of exams, and the average grade. Sort chronologically.
hint: DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date) truncates the date to the first day of that month — ideal for grouping by period. Add ::DATE for a readable display.
solution:
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)::DATE AS exam_month,
  COUNT(*)                             AS exam_count,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)                 AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)
ORDER BY exam_month;

title: Classify exams by academic exam period
description: For each year-month, combine EXTRACT and CASE WHEN to determine the exam session: month 1 = 'January session', month 6 = 'June session', month 9 = 'September session'. Show the session, the number of exams, and the average grade.
hint: Group by DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date), and in the SELECT add a CASE on EXTRACT(MONTH FROM ...) for the session label.
solution:
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)::DATE AS period_start,
  CASE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date))::INT
    WHEN 1 THEN 'January session'
    WHEN 6 THEN 'June session'
    WHEN 9 THEN 'September session'
    ELSE        'Other period'
  END AS exam_period,
  COUNT(*)             AS exam_count,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)
ORDER BY period_start;

title: Days between exams per student (LAG on date)
description: For each student, show every exam, the date of their previous exam (LAG), and the number of days between them. For the first exam there's no previous date — it will be NULL.
hint: LAG(exam_date) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) returns the date of the previous exam. Subtracting two DATE values gives an INTEGER (number of days).
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  exam_date,
  grade,
  LAG(exam_date) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) AS prev_exam_date,
  exam_date
    - LAG(exam_date) OVER(PARTITION BY student_id ORDER BY exam_date) AS days_between
FROM exams
ORDER BY student_id, exam_date;

title: First and last exam date per student
description: For each student who has an exam, show the first name, last name, the date of their first exam, the date of their last exam, and how many days they were active. Sort by number of days in descending order.
hint: MIN(e.exam_date) and MAX(e.exam_date) with GROUP BY. The difference between two DATE values is an INTEGER — the number of days.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  MIN(e.exam_date)                AS first_exam,
  MAX(e.exam_date)                AS last_exam,
  MAX(e.exam_date) - MIN(e.exam_date) AS days_active
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name
ORDER BY days_active DESC, s.last_name;

title: Student full name with CONCAT
description: Show each student's full name as a single column full_name using the CONCAT function. Also show the index number. Sort by last name.
hint: CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) joins the strings. Difference from the || operator: CONCAT ignores NULL values.
solution:
SELECT
  CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name,
  index_number
FROM students
ORDER BY last_name, first_name;

title: Student full name with year using ||
description: Show the student's full name and enrollment year in the format 'First Last (2022)' using the || operator. Sort by last name.
hint: || operator: first_name || ' ' || last_name || ' (' || enrollment_year::TEXT || ')'. To concatenate a number with text, use ::TEXT or CAST.
solution:
SELECT
  first_name || ' ' || last_name
    || ' (' || enrollment_year::TEXT || ')' AS full_name_with_year
FROM students
ORDER BY last_name, first_name;

title: Faculty names in uppercase
description: Show the names of all faculties in uppercase, together with the city.
hint: UPPER(name) converts all characters of the string to uppercase.
solution:
SELECT
  UPPER(name) AS faculty_name_upper,
  city
FROM faculties
ORDER BY name;

title: Subject names in lowercase
description: Show the names of all subjects in lowercase, together with the ECTS credits and semester.
hint: LOWER(name) converts all characters to lowercase.
solution:
SELECT
  LOWER(name) AS subject_name_lower,
  ects,
  semester
FROM subjects
ORDER BY name;

title: Length of subject names
description: Show the name of each subject and the length of the name in characters. Sort from longest to shortest.
hint: LENGTH(name) returns the number of characters in a string.
solution:
SELECT
  name,
  LENGTH(name) AS name_length
FROM subjects
ORDER BY name_length DESC, name;

title: TRIM demonstration with padded strings
description: Demonstrate TRIM: for each subject name, show the original name, the same name with spaces added on both sides, and a version with the spaces removed. Also show the difference in length.
hint: TRIM(string) removes leading and trailing spaces. We build '  ' || name || '  ' to add spaces, then TRIM to remove them.
solution:
SELECT
  name,
  '  ' || name || '  '         AS padded,
  TRIM('  ' || name || '  ')   AS trimmed_back,
  LENGTH('  ' || name || '  ')
    - LENGTH(TRIM('  ' || name || '  ')) AS chars_removed
FROM subjects
LIMIT 5;

title: City abbreviation with SUBSTRING
description: For each faculty, show the name, the city, and a city abbreviation — the first 3 letters of the city. Sort by city.
hint: SUBSTRING(city FROM 1 FOR 3) or, equivalently, LEFT(city, 3) — both are valid PostgreSQL syntax.
solution:
SELECT
  name,
  city,
  SUBSTRING(city FROM 1 FOR 3) AS city_abbr
FROM faculties
ORDER BY city;

title: Extract username from professor email
description: For each professor, show the email, the position of the '@' character in the email address, and the username (the part of the email address before '@').
hint: POSITION('@' IN email) returns the numeric position. SUBSTRING(email FROM 1 FOR POSITION('@' IN email) - 1) extracts the part before '@'.
solution:
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  email,
  POSITION('@' IN email)                                 AS at_position,
