  SUBSTRING(email FROM 1 FOR POSITION('@' IN email) - 1) AS username
FROM professors
ORDER BY last_name;

title: Find subjects by keyword with ILIKE
description: Find all subjects whose name contains 'analiz' or 'algebr' (case-insensitive search). Show the name, ECTS, and semester.
hint: ILIKE performs a case-insensitive search. Combine multiple ILIKE conditions with OR. % is a wildcard for any sequence of characters.
solution:
SELECT name, ects, semester
FROM subjects
WHERE name ILIKE '%analiz%'
   OR name ILIKE '%algebr%'
ORDER BY name;

title: Professor display name with UPPER title
description: For each professor, create a column display_name in the format 'TITLE: First Last', e.g. 'DOCENT: Stefan Jovanović'. Sort by title, then by last name.
hint: Combine UPPER(title), the literal string ': ', and the || concatenation for the first and last name.
solution:
SELECT
  title,
  first_name,
  last_name,
  UPPER(title) || ': ' || first_name || ' ' || last_name AS display_name
FROM professors
ORDER BY title, last_name;

title: Faculty label with CONCAT
description: For each faculty, create a label in the format 'Name (City) — University'. Use CONCAT. Sort alphabetically by name.
hint: CONCAT(name, ' (', city, ') — ', university_name) — CONCAT can take more than 2 arguments.
solution:
SELECT
  CONCAT(name, ' (', city, ') — ', university_name) AS faculty_label
FROM faculties
ORDER BY name;

title: Categorize subjects by name length
description: For each subject, show the name, the length of the name, and a category: 10 characters or fewer = 'Short', 11-20 = 'Medium', more than 20 = 'Long'. Sort by length in descending order.
hint: Combine LENGTH(name) and CASE WHEN. LENGTH returns an integer that you can use both in CASE and for ORDER BY.
solution:
SELECT
  name,
  LENGTH(name) AS name_length,
  CASE
    WHEN LENGTH(name) <= 10 THEN 'Short'
    WHEN LENGTH(name) <= 20 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE                        'Long'
  END AS name_category
FROM subjects
ORDER BY name_length DESC, name;

title: Count rows in students table
description: How many students are there in the database? Show the total count, the count with a department, and the count without a department, in a single query.
hint: COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(department_id) counts only non-NULL values. The difference gives the number of NULLs.
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*)                 AS total_students,
  COUNT(department_id)     AS with_department,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id) AS without_department
FROM students;

title: Row count for all tables
description: Show the number of rows in each of the 7 database tables (students, professors, faculties, departments, subjects, exams, professor_subjects). Sort by number of rows in descending order.
hint: Use UNION ALL with SELECT 'table_name' AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count for each table.
solution:
SELECT 'students'           AS table_name, COUNT(*) AS row_count FROM students
UNION ALL
SELECT 'professors',                COUNT(*) FROM professors
UNION ALL
SELECT 'faculties',                 COUNT(*) FROM faculties
UNION ALL
SELECT 'departments',               COUNT(*) FROM departments
UNION ALL
SELECT 'subjects',                  COUNT(*) FROM subjects
UNION ALL
SELECT 'exams',                     COUNT(*) FROM exams
UNION ALL
SELECT 'professor_subjects',        COUNT(*) FROM professor_subjects
ORDER BY row_count DESC;

title: NULL department_id count in students
description: How many students have no department assigned? Show the total number of students, the number with a NULL department_id, and the percentage.
hint: COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id) gives the number of NULL values, because COUNT ignores NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id) AS null_dept_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * (COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id)) / COUNT(*),
    1
  ) AS null_pct
FROM students;

title: NULL department_id count in subjects
description: How many subjects have no department assigned? Show the total number of subjects, the number without a department, and the percentage.
hint: Same technique as for students: COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id). Both NULL counts show the actual department coverage.
solution:
SELECT
  COUNT(*) AS total_subjects,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id) AS without_dept,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * (COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id)) / COUNT(*),
    1
  ) AS pct_without_dept
FROM subjects;

title: COALESCE: students with department or fallback
description: Show all students with their names and department name. For students without a department, show 'No department' instead of NULL. Use COALESCE with LEFT JOIN. Sort by department, then by last name.
hint: LEFT JOIN keeps students without a department (giving NULL). COALESCE(d.name, 'No department') replaces NULL with a fallback value.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  COALESCE(d.name, 'No department') AS department
FROM students s
LEFT JOIN departments d ON s.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY department, s.last_name;

title: Grade distribution
description: Show the grade distribution: for each grade, show the number of exams and the percentage of the total number of exams. Sort by grade.
hint: GROUP BY grade, COUNT(*). For the percentage: ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM exams), 1).
solution:
SELECT
  grade,
  COUNT(*) AS exam_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(*) / (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM exams),
    1
  ) AS percentage
FROM exams
GROUP BY grade
ORDER BY grade;

title: ECTS distribution
description: Show the ECTS credit distribution: for each ECTS value, show the number of subjects and the total ECTS that value contributes. Sort by ECTS.
hint: GROUP BY ects. COUNT(*) counts the subjects, COUNT(*) * ects gives the total contribution.
solution:
SELECT
  ects,
  COUNT(*) AS subject_count,
  COUNT(*) * ects AS total_ects_contribution
FROM subjects
GROUP BY ects
ORDER BY ects;

title: Duplicate student names
description: Find first_name + last_name combinations that appear more than once in the students table — potential duplicates. Show the first name, last name, and number of occurrences.
hint: GROUP BY first_name, last_name, HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. If the result is empty, the database has no duplicates — that's a good sign.
solution:
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  COUNT(*) AS occurrences
FROM students
GROUP BY first_name, last_name
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
ORDER BY occurrences DESC, last_name;

title: Students without exams (NOT EXISTS)
description: Find students who haven't taken a single exam. Use NOT EXISTS. Show the first name, last name, index number, and enrollment year.
hint: WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM exams e WHERE e.student_id = s.id) — useful for data quality, since it shows students who should have exams.
solution:
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  index_number,
  enrollment_year
FROM students s
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM exams e
  WHERE e.student_id = s.id
)
ORDER BY last_name;

title: Subjects without exams (LEFT JOIN + IS NULL)
description: Find subjects that have never been examined, using LEFT JOIN. Show the subject name, ECTS, and semester.
hint: LEFT JOIN subjects with exams. Where there's no match, e.subject_id is NULL — those are the subjects with no exam at all. Note: if the query returns 0 rows, that means every subject in the database has at least one exam — that is the correct result.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  sub.semester
FROM subjects sub
LEFT JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
WHERE e.subject_id IS NULL
ORDER BY sub.name;

title: Departments without assigned subjects
description: Find departments that have no subject assigned to them (in the subjects.department_id table). Show the department name, location, and faculty name.
hint: LEFT JOIN departments with subjects ON d.id = sub.department_id. Where there's no match, sub.department_id is NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  d.name     AS department_name,
  d.location,
  f.name     AS faculty_name
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN subjects sub ON d.id = sub.department_id
INNER JOIN faculties f  ON d.faculty_id = f.id
WHERE sub.department_id IS NULL
ORDER BY f.name, d.name;

title: Faculties without departments
description: Find faculties that have no department registered in the departments table. Show the name and city.
hint: LEFT JOIN faculties with departments ON f.id = d.faculty_id. Where there's no match, d.faculty_id is NULL.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name,
  f.city
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN departments d ON f.id = d.faculty_id
WHERE d.faculty_id IS NULL
ORDER BY f.name;

title: CAST: grade display formatting
description: For the first 10 exams, show the grade as text in the format 'N/10' and a normalized grade (0.0 to 1.0, two decimal places). Use CAST to convert the type.
hint: CAST(grade AS TEXT) converts a smallint to a string for concatenation. CAST(grade AS NUMERIC) / 10 gives a decimal result.
solution:
SELECT
  student_id,
  subject_id,
  grade,
  CAST(grade AS TEXT) || '/10'          AS grade_display,
  ROUND(CAST(grade AS NUMERIC) / 10, 2) AS grade_normalized
FROM exams
ORDER BY grade DESC
LIMIT 10;

title: NULL percentage across nullable columns
description: Show a NULL-value analysis for all nullable columns: students.department_id, subjects.department_id, and professors.department_id. For each column, show the total number of rows, the number of NULLs, and the NULL percentage.
hint: Use UNION ALL with one SELECT per column. COUNT(*) - COUNT(column) gives the number of NULLs. ORDER BY null_pct DESC.
solution:
SELECT
  'students.department_id'   AS nullable_column,
  COUNT(*)                   AS total_rows,
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id)  AS null_count,
  ROUND(100.0 * (COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id)) / COUNT(*), 1) AS null_pct
FROM students
UNION ALL
SELECT
  'subjects.department_id',
  COUNT(*),
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id),
  ROUND(100.0 * (COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id)) / COUNT(*), 1)
FROM subjects
UNION ALL
SELECT
  'professors.department_id',
  COUNT(*),
  COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id),
  ROUND(100.0 * (COUNT(*) - COUNT(department_id)) / COUNT(*), 1)
FROM professors
ORDER BY null_pct DESC;

title: Data quality summary
description: Build a comprehensive database quality overview: check 6 potential issues and show the number of affected records for each. Sort by number of issues in descending order.
hint: Use UNION ALL to combine 6 checks into a single result. Each SELECT checks a different data quality issue using IS NULL or a NOT IN subquery.
solution:
SELECT 'Students without a department' AS issue, COUNT(*) AS count FROM students   WHERE department_id IS NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Subjects without a department', COUNT(*) FROM subjects   WHERE department_id IS NULL
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Students without exams',        COUNT(*) FROM students   WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT student_id   FROM exams)
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Subjects without exams',        COUNT(*) FROM subjects   WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT subject_id    FROM exams)
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Professors without subjects',   COUNT(*) FROM professors WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT professor_id  FROM professor_subjects)
UNION ALL
SELECT 'Faculties without departments', COUNT(*) FROM faculties  WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT faculty_id   FROM departments)
ORDER BY count DESC, issue;

title: Project: Average grade per student
description: For every student who has taken at least one exam, show their first name, last name, index number, total number of exams, and average grade. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: JOIN students and exams, GROUP BY the student ID and all columns in the SELECT, ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) for the average.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  s.index_number,
  COUNT(*)               AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name, s.index_number
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC, s.last_name;

title: Passed and failed exams per student
description: For each student who has taken exams, show the first name, last name, total number of exams, number passed, and number failed. Use CASE WHEN inside COUNT. Sort by last name.
hint: COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END) only counts rows where the condition is met. Combine it with INNER JOIN and GROUP BY.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  COUNT(*)                                    AS total_exams,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END)        AS passed_count,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN NOT e.passed THEN 1 END)    AS failed_count
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name
ORDER BY s.last_name, s.first_name;

title: Project: Students with average grade above 8
description: Show only students whose average exam grade is greater than 8. Show their first name, last name, index number, average grade, and total number of exams. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: HAVING AVG(e.grade) > 8 filters groups after GROUP BY. The average is computed over all of the student's exams.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  s.index_number,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
  COUNT(*)               AS total_exams
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name, s.index_number
HAVING AVG(e.grade) > 8
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Student ranking by average grade
description: Rank all students who have taken exams by average grade, from best to worst. Show the first name, last name, average grade, total number of exams, and rank. Students with an equal average get the same rank.
hint: A CTE computes the averages per student. The outer SELECT applies RANK() OVER(ORDER BY avg_grade DESC) to that CTE result.
solution:
WITH student_avg AS (
  SELECT
    s.id,
    s.first_name,
    s.last_name,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade,
    COUNT(*)               AS total_exams
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name
)
SELECT
  first_name,
  last_name,
  avg_grade,
  total_exams,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY avg_grade DESC) AS grade_rank
FROM student_avg
ORDER BY grade_rank, last_name;

title: Best student per department
description: For each department, find the student with the highest average grade. Show the department name, the student's first and last name, and the average grade. If several students share the maximum, show all of them.
hint: The student_avg CTE computes the average per student (only students with a department). The dept_best CTE takes MAX(avg_grade) per department. The final JOIN filters students at the maximum.
solution:
WITH student_avg AS (
  SELECT
    s.id,
    s.first_name,
    s.last_name,
    s.department_id,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  WHERE s.department_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name, s.department_id
),
dept_best AS (
  SELECT department_id, MAX(avg_grade) AS best_avg
  FROM student_avg
  GROUP BY department_id
)
SELECT
  d.name        AS department_name,
  sa.first_name,
  sa.last_name,
  sa.avg_grade
FROM student_avg sa
INNER JOIN dept_best db
  ON sa.department_id = db.department_id
  AND sa.avg_grade    = db.best_avg
INNER JOIN departments d ON sa.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY d.name, sa.last_name;

title: Student performance summary
description: For each student who has exams, show: first name, last name, department, total number of exams, average grade, number passed, pass rate, and performance (Excellent/Very good/Good/Sufficient). Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: INNER JOIN exams, LEFT JOIN departments (a student doesn't have to have a department). COUNT(CASE WHEN passed...) for passed exams. CASE WHEN over AVG(grade) for the performance category.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  COALESCE(d.name, 'No department')                AS department_name,
  COUNT(*)                                          AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2)                            AS avg_grade,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END)              AS passed_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END) / COUNT(*),
    1
  )                                                 AS pass_rate_pct,
  CASE
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 9.0 THEN 'Excellent'
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 7.5 THEN 'Very good'
    WHEN AVG(e.grade) >= 6.5 THEN 'Good'
    ELSE                          'Sufficient'
  END                                               AS performance
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e        ON s.id           = e.student_id
LEFT  JOIN departments d  ON s.department_id = d.id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name, d.name
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC, s.last_name;

title: Number of departments per faculty
description: For every faculty, show its name, city, and the number of departments that belong to it. Include faculties with no departments too (count = 0). Sort by number of departments in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN faculties with departments to include faculties with no departments too. COUNT(d.id) — not COUNT(*) — counts only matched rows.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name          AS faculty_name,
  f.city,
  COUNT(d.id)     AS num_departments
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN departments d ON f.id = d.faculty_id
GROUP BY f.id, f.name, f.city
ORDER BY num_departments DESC, f.name;

title: Number of students per faculty
description: For every faculty, show its name, city, and the number of students studying there. Include faculties with no students too. Sort by number of students in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN faculties with students using f.id = s.faculty_id. COUNT(s.id) counts only students that actually exist.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name          AS faculty_name,
  f.city,
  COUNT(s.id)     AS num_students
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN students s ON f.id = s.faculty_id
GROUP BY f.id, f.name, f.city
ORDER BY num_students DESC, f.name;

title: Number of subjects per faculty
description: For every faculty, show its name, city, number of subjects, and total ECTS offered at that faculty. Include faculties with no subjects too. Sort by number of subjects in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN faculties with subjects. COUNT(sub.id) and COALESCE(SUM(sub.ects), 0) for the case where there are no subjects.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name                        AS faculty_name,
  f.city,
  COUNT(sub.id)                 AS num_subjects,
  COALESCE(SUM(sub.ects), 0)    AS total_ects
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN subjects sub ON f.id = sub.faculty_id
GROUP BY f.id, f.name, f.city
ORDER BY num_subjects DESC, f.name;

title: Average grade per faculty
description: For every faculty that has students with recorded exams, show its name, city, the number of distinct students who have taken exams, the total number of exams, and the average grade. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: JOIN chain: faculties → students (faculty_id) → exams (student_id). COUNT(DISTINCT s.id) for the number of students, AVG(e.grade) for the average.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name                       AS faculty_name,
  f.city,
  COUNT(DISTINCT s.id)         AS num_students_examined,
  COUNT(*)                     AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2)       AS avg_grade
FROM faculties f
INNER JOIN students s ON f.id = s.faculty_id
INNER JOIN exams e    ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY f.id, f.name, f.city
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Project: Faculties without departments
description: Find faculties that have no department registered in the departments table. Show the name, city, and university name.
hint: LEFT JOIN faculties with departments. Where there's no match, d.faculty_id is NULL — those are the faculties with no departments.
solution:
SELECT
  f.name,
  f.city,
  f.university_name
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN departments d ON f.id = d.faculty_id
WHERE d.faculty_id IS NULL
ORDER BY f.name;

title: Project: Faculty summary
description: For each faculty, show: name, city, number of departments, number of students, number of subjects, and average exam grade. Use multiple CTEs to avoid a Cartesian product. Sort by number of students in descending order.
hint: Each CTE computes one metric per faculty_id (dept_count, student_count, subject_count, exam_stats). The final SELECT LEFT JOINs all the CTEs with faculties.
solution:
WITH dept_count AS (
  SELECT faculty_id, COUNT(*) AS num_departments
  FROM departments
  GROUP BY faculty_id
),
student_count AS (
  SELECT faculty_id, COUNT(*) AS num_students
  FROM students
  GROUP BY faculty_id
),
subject_count AS (
  SELECT faculty_id, COUNT(*) AS num_subjects
  FROM subjects
  GROUP BY faculty_id
),
exam_stats AS (
  SELECT s.faculty_id,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
  FROM students s
  INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
  GROUP BY s.faculty_id
)
SELECT
  f.name                          AS faculty_name,
  f.city,
  COALESCE(dc.num_departments, 0) AS num_departments,
  COALESCE(sc.num_students, 0)    AS num_students,
  COALESCE(su.num_subjects, 0)    AS num_subjects,
  es.avg_grade
FROM faculties f
LEFT JOIN dept_count    dc ON f.id = dc.faculty_id
LEFT JOIN student_count sc ON f.id = sc.faculty_id
LEFT JOIN subject_count su ON f.id = su.faculty_id
LEFT JOIN exam_stats    es ON f.id = es.faculty_id
ORDER BY COALESCE(sc.num_students, 0) DESC;

title: Average grade per subject
description: For every subject that has been examined, show its name, ECTS, total number of attempts, and average grade. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: INNER JOIN subjects and exams (only subjects that have been examined). GROUP BY sub.id and all SELECT columns, ROUND(AVG, 2) for the average.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name               AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  COUNT(*)               AS total_attempts,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name, sub.ects
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Pass rate per subject
description: For each subject that has been examined, show the name, total number of attempts, number of exams passed, and the pass rate rounded to one decimal place. Sort by pass rate in descending order.
hint: COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END) counts the passed ones. Percentage: 100.0 * passed / total. Multiply by 100.0 (not 100) for a decimal result.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name                                          AS subject_name,
  COUNT(*)                                          AS total_attempts,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END)              AS passed_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END) / COUNT(*),
    1
  )                                                 AS pass_rate_pct
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name
ORDER BY pass_rate_pct DESC;

title: Number of exam attempts per subject
description: For every subject, show its name, ECTS, semester, total number of exam attempts, and the number of distinct students who took it. Sort by total number of attempts in descending order.
hint: COUNT(*) gives the total number of rows (attempts), COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) gives the number of unique students.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name                     AS subject_name,
  sub.ects,
  sub.semester,
  COUNT(*)                     AS total_attempts,
  COUNT(DISTINCT e.student_id) AS unique_students
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name, sub.ects, sub.semester
ORDER BY total_attempts DESC;

title: Hardest subjects by pass rate
description: Show subjects sorted from hardest (lowest pass rate) to easiest. For each subject, show its name, number of attempts, pass rate percentage, and average grade.
hint: Same query as the pass rate one, but ORDER BY pass_rate_pct ASC so the hardest subjects come first. Add avg_grade as a secondary criterion.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name                  AS subject_name,
  COUNT(*)                  AS total_attempts,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END) / COUNT(*),
    1
  )                         AS pass_rate_pct,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2)    AS avg_grade
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name
ORDER BY pass_rate_pct ASC, avg_grade ASC;

title: Subjects with most failed exams
description: Show subjects sorted by the absolute number of failed exams in descending order. For each subject, show the name, total number of attempts, number failed, and the fail rate (%).
hint: COUNT(CASE WHEN NOT e.passed THEN 1 END) counts the failed ones. Sorting by failed_count in descending order shows the subjects with the most failures.
solution:
SELECT
  sub.name                                        AS subject_name,
  COUNT(*)                                        AS total_attempts,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN NOT e.passed THEN 1 END)        AS failed_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(CASE WHEN NOT e.passed THEN 1 END) / COUNT(*),
    1
  )                                               AS fail_rate_pct
FROM subjects sub
INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name
ORDER BY failed_count DESC, fail_rate_pct DESC;

title: Subject difficulty classification
description: For each subject that has been examined, compute the average grade and pass rate, then classify it: Easy (avg >= 8 and pass rate >= 75%), Medium (avg >= 7 and pass rate >= 55%), otherwise Hard. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: The subject_stats CTE computes all the metrics per subject. The final SELECT adds a CASE WHEN classification based on avg_grade and pass_rate_pct from the CTE.
solution:
WITH subject_stats AS (
  SELECT
    sub.name                  AS subject_name,
    sub.ects,
    COUNT(*)                  AS total_attempts,
    ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2)    AS avg_grade,
    ROUND(
      100.0 * COUNT(CASE WHEN e.passed THEN 1 END) / COUNT(*),
      1
    )                         AS pass_rate_pct
  FROM subjects sub
  INNER JOIN exams e ON sub.id = e.subject_id
  GROUP BY sub.id, sub.name, sub.ects
)
SELECT
  subject_name,
  ects,
  total_attempts,
  avg_grade,
  pass_rate_pct,
  CASE
    WHEN avg_grade >= 8.0 AND pass_rate_pct >= 75 THEN 'Easy'
    WHEN avg_grade >= 7.0 AND pass_rate_pct >= 55 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE 'Hard'
  END AS difficulty_class
FROM subject_stats
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Project: Number of subjects per professor
description: For every professor, show their title, first name, last name, and the number of subjects assigned to them. Include professors with no subjects too (count = 0). Sort by number of subjects in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN professors with professor_subjects. COUNT(ps.subject_id) — not COUNT(*) — so professors with no subjects get 0.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  COUNT(ps.subject_id) AS num_subjects
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN professor_subjects ps ON p.id = ps.professor_id
GROUP BY p.id, p.title, p.first_name, p.last_name
ORDER BY num_subjects DESC, p.last_name;

title: Professors without subjects
description: Find professors who have no subject assigned in the professor_subjects table. Show their title, first name, last name, and email.
hint: LEFT JOIN professors with professor_subjects. Where there's no match, ps.professor_id is NULL — those are the professors with no subjects.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  p.email
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN professor_subjects ps ON p.id = ps.professor_id
WHERE ps.professor_id IS NULL
ORDER BY p.last_name, p.first_name;

title: Number of exams per professor
description: For every professor, show their title, first name, last name, and the number of exams they've graded. Include professors who haven't graded any exam too. Sort by number of exams in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN professors with exams using p.id = e.professor_id. COUNT(e.grade) counts only non-NULL values.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  COUNT(e.grade) AS num_exams
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN exams e ON p.id = e.professor_id
GROUP BY p.id, p.title, p.first_name, p.last_name
ORDER BY num_exams DESC, p.last_name;

title: Average grade by professor
description: For every professor who has graded at least one exam, show their title, first name, last name, total number of exams, and the average grade they've given. Sort by average grade in descending order.
hint: INNER JOIN professors and exams (only professors with exams). GROUP BY professor, ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2).
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  COUNT(*)               AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(e.grade), 2) AS avg_grade
FROM professors p
INNER JOIN exams e ON p.id = e.professor_id
GROUP BY p.id, p.title, p.first_name, p.last_name
ORDER BY avg_grade DESC;

title: Professor workload category
description: For each professor, show the title, first name, last name, number of assigned subjects, and a workload category: 0 subjects = 'No subjects', 1-2 = 'Low', 3-4 = 'Medium', 5+ = 'High'. Sort by number of subjects in descending order.
hint: LEFT JOIN + GROUP BY for the subject count. CASE WHEN COUNT(ps.subject_id) = 0 THEN ... in the SELECT classifies each row.
solution:
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  COUNT(ps.subject_id) AS num_subjects,
  CASE
    WHEN COUNT(ps.subject_id) = 0  THEN 'No subjects'
    WHEN COUNT(ps.subject_id) <= 2 THEN 'Low'
    WHEN COUNT(ps.subject_id) <= 4 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE 'High'
  END AS workload_category
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN professor_subjects ps ON p.id = ps.professor_id
GROUP BY p.id, p.title, p.first_name, p.last_name
ORDER BY num_subjects DESC, p.last_name;

title: Professor workload summary
description: For each professor, show: title, first name, last name, department, number of assigned subjects, number of graded exams, and average grade. Use CTEs for clean code. Sort by number of subjects in descending order.
hint: The prof_subjects CTE counts subjects per professor. The prof_exams CTE counts exams and computes the average. The final SELECT LEFT JOINs both CTEs with professors and departments.
solution:
WITH prof_subjects AS (
  SELECT professor_id, COUNT(*) AS num_subjects
  FROM professor_subjects
  GROUP BY professor_id
),
prof_exams AS (
  SELECT professor_id,
    COUNT(*)               AS num_exams,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)   AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY professor_id
)
SELECT
  p.title,
  p.first_name,
  p.last_name,
  COALESCE(d.name, 'No department') AS department_name,
  COALESCE(ps.num_subjects, 0)       AS num_subjects,
  COALESCE(pe.num_exams, 0)          AS num_exams,
  pe.avg_grade
FROM professors p
LEFT JOIN departments   d  ON p.department_id = d.id
LEFT JOIN prof_subjects ps ON p.id            = ps.professor_id
LEFT JOIN prof_exams    pe ON p.id            = pe.professor_id
ORDER BY num_subjects DESC, p.last_name;

title: Exams by year
description: Group all exams by year. For each year, show the year, the total number of exams, the number of distinct students who took them, and the average grade. Sort chronologically.
hint: EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date)::INT for the year. GROUP BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date). COUNT(DISTINCT student_id) for unique students.
solution:
SELECT
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date)::INT AS exam_year,
  COUNT(*)                          AS total_exams,
  COUNT(DISTINCT student_id)        AS unique_students,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)              AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY EXTRACT(YEAR FROM exam_date)
ORDER BY exam_year;

title: Exams by month
description: Group exams by year and month using DATE_TRUNC. For each month, show the month (as the date of the first day), the total number of exams, and the average grade. Sort chronologically.
hint: DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)::DATE truncates the date to the first day of the month — ideal for grouping by period.
solution:
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)::DATE AS exam_month,
  COUNT(*)                             AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)                 AS avg_grade
FROM exams
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)
ORDER BY exam_month;

title: Average grade by month with pass rate
description: For each month, show: the month (date of the first day), the number of exams, the average grade, the number passed, and the pass rate. Sort chronologically. Combines DATE_TRUNC, AVG, and CASE WHEN.
hint: GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date). Add COUNT(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 END) for the passed count and compute the percentage with 100.0 * passed / total.
solution:
SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)::DATE AS exam_month,
  COUNT(*)                             AS total_exams,
  ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)                 AS avg_grade,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 END)   AS passed_count,
  ROUND(
    100.0 * COUNT(CASE WHEN passed THEN 1 END) / COUNT(*),
    1
  )                                    AS pass_rate_pct
FROM exams
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)
ORDER BY exam_month;

title: First and last exam per student
description: For each student who has exams, show the first name, last name, the date of their first exam, the date of their last exam, the number of days they were active, and the total number of exams. Sort by number of active days in descending order.
hint: MIN(e.exam_date) and MAX(e.exam_date) in GROUP BY. Subtracting two DATE values in PostgreSQL returns an INTEGER (number of days).
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  MIN(e.exam_date)                        AS first_exam_date,
  MAX(e.exam_date)                        AS last_exam_date,
  MAX(e.exam_date) - MIN(e.exam_date)     AS days_active,
  COUNT(*)                                AS total_exams
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
GROUP BY s.id, s.first_name, s.last_name
ORDER BY days_active DESC, s.last_name;

title: Days between student exams
description: For each exam, show the student's first and last name, the exam date, the grade, the date of that student's previous exam, and the number of days between those two exams. For the first exam there's no previous data (NULL). Sort by last name, then by date.
hint: LAG(e.exam_date) OVER(PARTITION BY e.student_id ORDER BY e.exam_date) returns the date of the previous exam. Subtracting DATE values gives the number of days as an INTEGER.
solution:
SELECT
  s.first_name,
  s.last_name,
  e.exam_date,
  e.grade,
  LAG(e.exam_date) OVER (
    PARTITION BY e.student_id ORDER BY e.exam_date
  )                     AS prev_exam_date,
  e.exam_date - LAG(e.exam_date) OVER (
    PARTITION BY e.student_id ORDER BY e.exam_date
  )                     AS days_between
FROM students s
INNER JOIN exams e ON s.id = e.student_id
ORDER BY s.last_name, s.first_name, e.exam_date;

title: Monthly exam trend with running total
description: Show the monthly exam trend: for each month, show the number of exams, the average grade, and the cumulative running total of exams up to that month. Use a CTE + SUM() OVER(ORDER BY).
hint: The monthly_exams CTE groups by DATE_TRUNC('month', ...). The outer SELECT applies SUM(monthly_count) OVER(ORDER BY exam_month) for the running total.
solution:
WITH monthly_exams AS (
  SELECT
    DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)::DATE AS exam_month,
    COUNT(*)                             AS monthly_count,
    ROUND(AVG(grade), 2)                 AS avg_grade
  FROM exams
  GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', exam_date)
)
SELECT
  exam_month,
  monthly_count,
  avg_grade,
  SUM(monthly_count) OVER (ORDER BY exam_month) AS running_total
FROM monthly_exams
ORDER BY exam_month;
